Fig. 1. shows how desalination costs are broken down into two categories: capital costs and operation and maintenance costs. Indirect costs (such as LEED design, engineering, supervision, and construction overheads) and direct expenditures (such as power, brine disposal systems, and process equipment) are examples of capital costs. The expenses of operations and maintenance are divided into two categories: variable costs (such as labour, energy, chemicals, and spare parts) and fixed costs (such as insurance and amortisation). This framework allows for a thorough analysis of the overall water cost per cubic meter ($/m 3), considering operational and financial aspects.([Shokri & Sanavi Fard, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0555)).

The present review presents a comprehensive analysis of the prevailing desalination technologies methodically. This text focuses on the research advancement of TD and, MBD and discusses their functioning principles. An analysis is conducted on the economic/cost benefits and environmental consequences of SD, as well as the evaluation of various SD methodologies, to predict the future possibilities of desalination technology. This review aims to establish a theoretical foundation for the choice and application of SD technology as a powerful weapon for the future.

Existing desalination technologies
----------------------------------

Desalination is a method for acquiring fresh water from seawater due to increased demand and decreased reserves. It divides saline input water into two streams, permeating fresh water with a TDS of less than 500 mg/L and rejecting brine-containing concentrated TDS. ([Elewa, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0165), [Sahu, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0530)).

### Technologies that rely on membranes

MBD technologies are technologies that do not involve phase transitions and differ from TD technologies in that they employ filtering saline water streams using selective removal of dissolved salts by semi-permeable membranes, rather than relying on temperature changes. The process of separating substances involves the use of external pressure, usually with the help of electrical energy, to counteract osmotic pressure and force water to move across a membrane while keeping the salts behind. The membrane technologies that have been installed on the largest scale include electrodialysis (ED) ([Naim et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0430)), reverse osmosis (RO) and nanofiltration (NF) ([Naim et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0435)). In contrast, other technologies like forward osmosis (FO) ([Mohammadifakhr et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0405)), pervaporation (PEV) ([Elsheniti et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0185)), membrane distillation (MD), ED reversal (EDR), ED metathesis (EDM) ([Chen et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0110)), liquid membrane (LM) ([Moneer et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0420)), membrane crystallisation (MCr) ([Ting et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0580)) and capacitive deionization (CDI) ([Elewa et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0170)) are promising technologies. These technologies have given rise to many variations that aim to enhance treatment efficiency and address a wide range of impacts ([Cipolletta et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0130), [Panagopoulos et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0465)). RO is an exceptional desalination method known for its minimal energy usage (3–5 kWh m−3) and ability to reclaim as much as 50% of freshwater ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#f0010)a). During this procedure, the application of pressure results in the passage of freshwater (known as permeate) through a partially permeable membrane, leading to the concentration of all salts in the brine that was rejected. Similar to other MBD, the primary challenges associated with this technique are membrane fouling issues and the frequent necessity of costly pre-treatments ([Charcosset, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0105)). Osmosis is the driving force behind FO, which is a naturally occurring phenomenon that provides the impression of being more energy-efficient, with energy usage ranging from 0.8 to 13 kWh m−3 and has the capability to recover up to 98% of freshwater ([Ahmad et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0015)). High-pressure RO (HPRO) is a cost-effective solution that requires minimal energy consumption (3–9 kWh m−3). It can be used for brine treatment salinity levels up to twice that of traditional RO systems (120–140 g/L TDS) by utilising increased pressure ([Subramani et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0570)). This is made possible by additional choices, such as osmotically assisted RO (OARO) ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#f0010)b), which allows for the sequential utilisation of multiple stages of RO and forward osmosis. When applied to the permeate side, as a sweep flow, OARO makes use of a salty stream that has a salinity that is either lower than or equal to that of the permeate. The osmotic pressure difference that exists between the membrane's two sides is decreased, as a result of this, which makes it possible for water to be transported even in situations when the feed's osmotic pressure is lower than the hydraulic pressure that comes from the outside ([Liang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0370)). HPRO and OARO are viable substitutes for conventional RO. Increased water transport and reduced energy consumption are made possible by their potential to reduce the osmotic pressure gradient across the membrane. To achieve the effective advancement of these technologies, it is necessary to have robust and porous membranes that can endure elevated pressures and high levels of salt concentration. Several investigations have successfully produced membranes made of thin-film composite (TFC) that exhibit exceptional hydraulic burst resistance, reaching up to 110 bars. These membranes also demonstrate a remarkable NaCl rejection rate of over a high percentage of pure water and a 98 percent purity level of around 3 kg m-2 h−1 bar−1 ([Liang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0370)). Ultimately, HPRO and OARO hold promise for enhancing energy efficiency and water retrieval in the desalination and treatment of briny waters. However, further investigation and advancement in the development and production of appropriate membranes for these procedures are necessary. To reduce the risk of membrane fouling, many membrane technologies need to undergo a pretreatment. Membrane fouling is the process of undesired chemicals building up within a membrane's pores or on its surface, resulting in decreased permeability and performance. The system's performance is influenced by factors such as the quality of feedwater, operating conditions, and the membrane's properties. Effective pretreatment techniques such as disinfection, filtration, flocculation, and coagulation are essential for minimizing membrane fouling in feedwater, decreasing solid particles, and enhancing efficiency and energy requirements. To ensure water purity and membrane protection, additional equipment, pretreatment devices, monitoring, and chemical application are needed. However, extensive pretreatment may introduce contaminants or generate waste streams ([Nthunya et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0445)). A potential alternative to exerting pressure in membrane technologies involves the application of direct current voltage to create a potential difference. Hence, ions with positive and negative charges move across specific membranes, with protons and anions migrating, respectively ([Peters & Hankins, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0480)). ED and its derivatives, including microbial desalination cells (MDC), EDM, and EDR are all examples of the kind of processes that exhibit these phenomena ([Prado de Nicolás, Molina-García, García-Bermejo, & Vera-García, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0495)). ED ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#f0010)c) efficiently reuses water, reduces waste, eliminates chemicals, and effectively removes non-ionic and ionic constituents like heavy metals, dyes, nutrients, and organic molecules. Depending on the kind of ion-exchange membranes (IEMs) that are used and the number of them, it is possible to attain TDS concentrations that are close to zero. Additionally, ED has minimal energy usage and operational expenses, rendering it a financially feasible choice for water treatment purposes. Nevertheless, it is important to take into account certain restrictions. ED is insufficient in eliminating pathogens, viruses, and bacteria, thus requiring other measures to guarantee the microbiological purity of water. Furthermore, the system encounters difficulties in treating extremely saline fluids due to the amplified electrical resistance and voltage loss, which might adversely impact its effectiveness. Finally, the widespread use of ED may be limited by the availability and expense of IEMs that have good selectivity, permeability, and chemical/mechanical stability ([Bello et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0065), [Morillo et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0425)). MD is an innovative membrane process that operates at different temperatures ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#f0010)d). It makes use of the variation in partial pressure on the two surfaces of a microporous membrane that is hydrophobic to facilitate the process by which molecules of water vapour are transferred from the input stream to the permeate ([Qtaishat & Banat, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0510)). There are multiple benefits associated with MD. Firstly, it enables the production of water with excellent permeability and high-water rejection. Like ED, MD does not necessitate the utilisation of chemicals and produces no deleterious by-products. It can effectively treat waters with high salinity levels (>70 g/L), which are difficult to treat using alternative methods like RO. Furthermore, MD demonstrates a notable advantage in terms of energy efficiency and operational expenses, as it has the capability to harness either waste heat or heat sources that are renewable ([Duong et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0150)). Avoiding membrane wetting and fouling, which can reduce process longevity and efficiency, requires proper pre-treatment. MD's effectiveness in eliminating volatile organic molecules is limited due to their ability to pollute membranes and the thermodynamic challenge of reaching TDS concentrations close to zero. The broad adoption of hydrophobic membranes with excellent permeability, porosity, and chemical and thermal stability could be limited by their availability and cost ([Julian et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0290)). To fully realise the promise of MD in different water treatment applications, it is imperative to tackle these problems. Membrane-based methods present benefits and drawbacks within the water treatment industry and desalination.

### Technologies that rely on heat as a primary mechanism

In TD systems, TDS is removed from saline water utilising external heat and energy, mimicking the natural water cycle, and resulting in freshwater formation. The thermal methods that have achieved the most commercial success are Multi-Effect Distillation (MED) and Multi-Stage Flash (MSF), ([Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#f0015)a). However, other technologies rely on brine concentration and crystallisation which are also becoming increasingly important ([Wei et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0615)).TD technologies, while generally competitive, are less lucrative and more energy-intensive than MBD solutions due to the considerable amount of energy they require and significant capital expenditure. Nevertheless, the prerequisites for pre-treatment are minor, and it is possible to treat brine with a wider range of TDS. In addition, TD methods are typically effective in treating small quantities of brine that have high levels of TDS and can achieve either minimal liquid discharge (MLD) or zero liquid discharge (ZLD). MSF and MED systems achieve a freshwater recovery of 85% while consuming 8–24 kWh m−3 of energy. These systems may treat influents with a TDS up to 180,000 mg/L ([Al-Sahali and Ettouney, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0035), [Subramani and Jacangelo, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1687428524001055#b0575)). One benefit of these arrangements is that multiple stages can be arranged sequentially to get a greater proportion of freshwater recovery. enriched_points.expanded_content: Smart water grids leverage IoT, AI, and real-time data analytics to enhance efficiency, reduce waste, and improve water management across urban infrastructure.
Integrating IoT sensors enables real-time monitoring of water quality and flow, facilitating proactive maintenance and reducing operational costs through data-driven decision-making.
Innovations like forward osmosis and membrane distillation promise lower energy consumption and effective brine management, enhancing the sustainability of desalination processes.
